title: The Foundation

jokes.text: Boy, that was awkward. I don't mind the cemetery. In fact, now I see why people like golf. It's just nice to be outside in a well-landscaped area.
jokes.setup: Expected emotional response to cemetery visit after fiancée's death
jokes.punchline: Comparing cemetery to golf course for landscaping quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - finding the most inappropriate positive spin on a cemetery visit. Classic Seinfeld observational humor with George's characteristic self-absorption.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like a golf course. You know, without the golfers. Just beautiful greens, and the way they maintain it... I was thinking, this is what golf courses should look like.

jokes.text: Her death takes place in the shadow of new life. She's not really dead if we find a way to remember her. / What is that? / Star Trek II. Wrath of Khan.
jokes.setup: George delivers what sounds like profound condolence wisdom to grieving parents
jokes.punchline: Revealed to be plagiarized from a sci-fi movie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reveal that George's seemingly heartfelt condolence is actually a movie quote. Perfect character work - George would absolutely do this. The delayed reveal makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm sorry for your loss. It's the saddest thing that's ever happened to you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, that's beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... I didn't know what to say. I was panicking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, it was perfect. Where'd you come up with that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I saw it in a movie. Alien. When Ripley's talking to the android.

jokes.text: They wrap him up in a towel and they shoot him out the bowels of the ship in that big sunglasses case.
jokes.setup: Describing Spock's funeral from Star Trek
jokes.punchline: Reducing iconic death scene to crude physical details
jokes.explanation: George's casual, irreverent description of a dramatic movie moment. Funny but not exceptional - typical Seinfeld deflation of pop culture moments.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I was watching that episode of Star Trek where Spock dies. And I'm thinking, this is supposed to be this big, meaningful moment. But really, what is it? It's just a guy lying there, not moving. That's it. That's death. We've made it into this whole thing, but it's really just... nothing happening.

jokes.text: I have mourned for three long months, summer months too. Anybody could grieve in January.
jokes.setup: George claiming he's done sufficient mourning
jokes.punchline: Suggesting summer mourning is more difficult than winter mourning
jokes.explanation: Perfect George self-centeredness - even his grief is about him and seasonal preferences. The logic is absurd but delivered with complete conviction.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I've done my mourning. I've cried, I've suffered. Now it's time to move on.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Besides, summer mourning is much harder than winter mourning. In the winter, you're already depressed anyway.

jokes.text: Karate is not here: It's here: And here:
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining karate philosophy to Jerry
jokes.punchline: Pointing to various body parts in mock-spiritual fashion
jokes.explanation: Standard Kramer pseudo-philosophy moment with physical gestures. Functional but predictable for the character.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You see, the key to karate is not the hands or the feet... it's the mind.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The body is merely a vessel for the warrior spirit within.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay, sensei.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This is the eye of the tiger. This is the heart of the lion. And this... this is the butt of the jackass.

jokes.text: I think you're wrong. We just went to the fireworks the other day. / That was July 4th.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying Elaine has been gone six weeks, Kramer disagreeing
jokes.punchline: Kramer's 'recent' memory is actually six weeks old
jokes.explanation: Clean setup-punchline about time perception. Well-structured but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She's been gone six weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Six weeks? No, no, she was just here.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, she left six weeks ago.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, that was... that was recent. That was recent.

jokes.text: When I get back I don't wanna see you here: Here or here:
jokes.setup: Jerry telling Kramer to leave
jokes.punchline: Mimicking Kramer's earlier karate pointing gestures
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Kramer's gestures, showing Jerry's sarcastic response to Kramer's philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You gotta go.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because I said so. Out!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: *makes exaggerated karate pointing gestures* Hiii-YAH!

jokes.text: Anything you couldn't have gotten tearing open a bag of Doritos and watching Viva Zapata?
jokes.setup: Elaine claiming she got tons of ideas in Mexico
jokes.punchline: Jerry suggesting she could have gotten same inspiration from snacks and old movies
jokes.explanation: Sharp, specific Jerry deflation of Elaine's work travel. The Doritos/Viva Zapata combination is perfectly absurd and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I got so many ideas in Mexico!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You could've gotten the same ideas from snacks and old movies.

jokes.text: All of a sudden we both just blurted out: I hate you. / I hate you. / See you. / See you.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing his breakup
jokes.punchline: The casual, simultaneous nature of mutual hatred declaration
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist take on breakups. The parallel structure and casual delivery make it both shocking and hilarious. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We both said 'I hate you' at the same time. It was like we were synchronized swimmers of animosity.

jokes.text: It was the first truly mutual breakup in relationship history. No rejection, no guilt, no remorse.
jokes.setup: Jerry analyzing his breakup
jokes.punchline: Claiming it's historically unprecedented
jokes.explanation: Jerry's typical over-analysis of relationships with grandiose claims. Good character work but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, this is the worst breakup in human history. No one has ever suffered like this before. I'm pretty sure they're going to have to rewrite the history books. 'Chapter 12: The Breakup of Jerry and Her — A Dark Time For Mankind.'

jokes.text: You've never felt remorse. / I know. I feel bad about that.
jokes.setup: Elaine pointing out Jerry never feels remorse
jokes.punchline: Jerry feeling bad about not feeling bad - a logical contradiction
jokes.explanation: Perfect paradox joke with excellent economy. Jerry's self-aware but still emotionally detached response is quintessential character work.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You never feel bad about anything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not true. I feel bad.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: About what?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: About not feeling bad.

jokes.text: I like to call it the 'urban sombrero.'
jokes.setup: Elaine pitching a hat idea combining Mexico and city panache
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous name 'urban sombrero'
jokes.explanation: Iconic Seinfeld creation - an absurd product name that becomes culturally memorable. Sets up major plot developments.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's a sombrero, but it's urban. It's like a regular sombrero, but it's got this sleek, metropolitan look to it. We could call it the 'urban sombrero.'

jokes.text: I wanna bite into a big hunk of cheese, just bite into it like it's an apple.
jokes.setup: Peterman describing his newfound autonomy
jokes.punchline: Wanting to eat cheese like fruit as ultimate freedom
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist image that perfectly captures Peterman's eccentric character. The specificity makes it memorable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I've discovered true autonomy. The ability to eat cheese like fruit—that's ultimate freedom.

jokes.text: It's Dolores. / Who? / Mulva.
jokes.setup: Jerry seeing someone he knows
jokes.punchline: Revealing Dolores is the infamous 'Mulva' from earlier episode
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to fan-favorite episode. The economy of the exchange is perfect - maximum payoff with minimum setup for those who know the reference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Dolores!

dialogue.speaker: Dolores
dialogue.line: Jerry! So good to see you!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, you too. It's been a while.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Who is that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's Dolores.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Dolores? Wait... isn't that...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Mulva.

jokes.text: I've shown I can go all the way. / All the way? / Not our all the way. Their all the way.
jokes.setup: George explaining his engagement appeal
jokes.punchline: Clarifying he means commitment, not sex
jokes.explanation: Solid innuendo joke with good clarification payoff. George's desperation to explain his appeal is in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm engaged. Engagement, it's a very big thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm committed. I'm a committed man.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: To Susan?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: To the idea of being engaged. The concept of engagement appeals to me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But not Susan.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, Susan too. But mainly the engagement thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what's the appeal? The sex?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no. The commitment. I like knowing that I'm committed to something. It makes me feel like an adult.

jokes.text: I got the stink of responsibility on me.
jokes.setup: George explaining his newfound dating appeal
jokes.punchline: Describing responsibility as a literal stink
jokes.explanation: Perfect George metaphor - making responsibility sound like a disease. Captures his aversion to commitment while explaining his new appeal.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I realized something. Women don't want a man who's trying too hard. They want a man who doesn't care. So I stopped caring. And now they love me.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like I have this... this aura of indifference. And responsibility? Responsibility is like a stink. Once you have it, you can't get rid of it.

jokes.text: My mind is as barren as the surface of the moon.
jokes.setup: Peterman explaining his burnout
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic metaphor for creative block
jokes.explanation: Classic Peterman over-the-top description. In character but not particularly original or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I'm creatively bankrupt. My reservoir of ideas has run dry. I'm like a well that's been drained of all its water — except the water was ideas, and the well was my brain.

jokes.text: You most likely know it as Myanmar. But it will always be Burma to me.
jokes.setup: Peterman saying he's in Burma
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the name change but preferring the old version
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Peterman's stubbornness and romantic view of the world. Specific and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: I'm in Burma.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Yes, I know it's now Myanmar, but I don't care.

jokes.text: You there, on the motorbike, sell me one of your melons.
jokes.setup: Peterman on phone from Burma
jokes.punchline: Randomly addressing local vendor mid-conversation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Peterman moment - completely distracted by exotic local culture mid-important phone call. Captures his scattered priorities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I understand. We'll need those fabrics by Thursday... You there, with the cart! Move it along!

dialogue.speaker: Peterman
dialogue.line: No, not you. Continue. Where was I? Right, the shipment...

jokes.text: Isn't it Myanmar now? / Jerry, he wants me to run the catalogue.
jokes.setup: Burma/Myanmar confusion
jokes.punchline: Elaine completely missing the point, focusing on bigger crisis
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Elaine's priorities. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, Burma? I thought it was called Myanmar now.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, they changed the name.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, that's just confusing. How is anyone supposed to know where anything is anymore?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, it's the same country, they just renamed it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's exactly my point! This is a huge problem. Do you know how many people are going to show up at the old Burma and it won't be there anymore?

jokes.text: Is that the discount pharmacy?
jokes.setup: Discussion about Myanmar
jokes.punchline: Kramer confusing country with pharmacy chain
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer confusion between similar-sounding things. In character but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Kramer, I heard you're going to Myanmar.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm thinking about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Myanmar? That's a country.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you said Rite Aid.

jokes.text: My legs, they were like noodles. Then I looked inside, and I found my katra.
jokes.setup: Kramer describing his first karate experience
jokes.punchline: Using Star Trek terminology for inner strength
jokes.explanation: Kramer mixing pop culture with pseudo-spirituality. Typical character behavior but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, this sensei, he's like a Jedi master. He says to me, 'Kramer, you must find your inner strength.' So I'm standing there in my gi, and he's going on about chakras and energy flow. I say, 'Sensei, I don't have any inner strength. I have inner weakness.' He looks at me like I just kicked him in the face. Which, by the way, I did. Accidentally.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: But then I realized something. My inner strength? It's gotta be like the Enterprise. You gotta engage the warp drive. Full impulse power.

jokes.text: Sammy Davis had it.
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining 'katra' - inner spirit
jokes.punchline: Random celebrity example of spiritual strength
jokes.explanation: Random Kramer reference that's specific enough to be funny. The unexpected choice of Sammy Davis Jr. works.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You see, the katra is like your inner spirit, your essence. It's what makes you... you.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Like, take someone like Marlon Brando. That guy had a powerful katra. You could feel it.

jokes.text: Pair of pants, stupid story, a huge markup. I can do that.
jokes.setup: Elaine claiming she can run Peterman's company
jokes.punchline: Reducing the business model to its cynical essentials
jokes.explanation: Sharp business satire that perfectly captures Peterman's model. Elaine's confident reduction is both accurate and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I could run this company. It's not that hard. You just tell people what they want to hear, make them feel good about themselves, and they'll buy anything.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Peterman
dialogue.line: That's... actually exactly what we do.

jokes.text: Kramer, you're fighting children?
jokes.setup: Revealing Kramer is in same class as a 9-year-old
jokes.punchline: Jerry's horrified realization about the situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff revealing Kramer's absurd karate situation. Jerry's reaction is the audience's reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, you're in the same class as a nine-year-old?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, so?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? Kramer, you're sitting in a classroom with a nine-year-old!

jokes.text: You don't need karate. You can just wring his neck.
jokes.setup: Jerry pointing out age advantage over 9-year-old
jokes.punchline: Suggesting violence against child as easier alternative
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about fighting children. Funny but relies somewhat on shock value rather than clever structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have an age advantage over a 9-year-old. I could take him. But why would I want to? What would I prove? That I can beat up a kid? I'm not interested in that kind of competition. Although, I gotta say, it would be easier.

jokes.text: Now, I want four new ideas from each of you by 6:00. No, make that six ideas by 4:00.
jokes.setup: Elaine taking charge of catalogue staff
jokes.punchline: Immediately changing demands to be more unreasonable
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's inexperience and overcompensation as new boss. Standard power-trip humor.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alright listen up everyone, I'm in charge now and things are going to change around here.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: From now on, I want everyone here by 7 AM sharp. And I want the coffee maker cleaned every hour on the hour.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Also, no talking. At all. Just work in complete silence.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, and one more thing — everyone has to wear the same color shirt. I'm thinking... chartreuse.

jokes.text: Yes, I agree. I hope my parents go long before I do.
jokes.setup: Rosses saying it's terrible when parents outlive children
jokes.punchline: George completely missing the emotional point, making it about himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - completely tone-deaf response that makes a tragedy about his own preferences. Peak self-absorption.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's awful. But you know what's worse? When you outlive your parents and they don't leave you any money.

jokes.text: He said to us, 'She's not really dead if her shadow is--' / What was it, dear? / Something about a way, wasn't it? And a light and a-- / Who the hell knows.
jokes.setup: Rosses trying to remember Jerry's 'inspiring' words
jokes.punchline: Completely mangling the Star Trek quote that started the foundation
jokes.explanation: Great callback showing how George's fake wisdom became garbled but still inspired action. The 'who the hell knows' is perfectly dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Ross
dialogue.line: Jerry told us something that really stuck with us.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Ross
dialogue.line: Yes, it was so inspiring.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Ross
dialogue.line: He said... 'To boldly go where no one has gone before.'

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Ross
dialogue.line: That's not what he said.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Ross
dialogue.line: He said something like that.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Ross
dialogue.line: He said, 'These pretzels are making me thirsty.'

jokes.text: That's the line that destroyed my life.
jokes.setup: Jerry repeating the Star Trek quote
jokes.punchline: George blaming movie quote for his current predicament
jokes.explanation: Perfect summation of how George's lazy plagiarism backfired completely. Great callback structure with emotional payoff.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is all because of that stupid movie quote. If I hadn't said 'Make it so,' I wouldn't be in this mess right now.

jokes.text: I was living the dream. Stripped to the waist, eating a block of cheese the size of a car battery.
jokes.setup: George describing his brief freedom
jokes.punchline: Defining paradise as shirtless cheese consumption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to Peterman's cheese fantasy with George's own twist. The car battery comparison is perfectly specific and absurd. Instant classic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is paradise. No shirt, no shoes, no service? I don't care. I'm eating cheese.

jokes.text: Don't you see? I'm back in.
jokes.setup: George explaining his foundation predicament
jokes.punchline: Using Godfather-style language about being trapped
jokes.explanation: Good use of movie reference to describe George's trap. The dramatic tone fits his tendency to melodrama.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm like a guy in The Godfather who tried to get out but they keep pulling me back in!

jokes.text: They had more than some monies. Many, many monies.
jokes.setup: George saying Rosses have 'some monies'
jokes.punchline: Wyck correcting with even more awkward plural
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay on awkward money plural. Functional character work but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: The Rosses have some monies.

dialogue.speaker: Wyck
dialogue.line: Monies? It's 'money.' You don't say 'monies.'

jokes.text: This can't be easy. / You know, it really can't.
jokes.setup: Wyck offering sympathy about what George lost
jokes.punchline: George's deadpan agreement about his own situation
jokes.explanation: George's matter-of-fact self-pity. Simple but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Wyck
dialogue.line: I know how you feel. I lost something too.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I lost my dignity. And my self-respect. And my ability to look people in the eye.

jokes.text: Everybody knows there's no such thing as a mutual breakup.
jokes.setup: George claiming his breakup was mutual
jokes.punchline: Mulva calling out the impossibility
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Jerry's breakup story, with universal relationship truth. Mulva being the voice of reason is ironic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was mutual. We both agreed to break up.

dialogue.speaker: Mulva
dialogue.line: Mutual? You called me up and said you never wanted to see me again!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, you didn't object.

dialogue.speaker: Mulva
dialogue.line: Because I couldn't get a word in!

jokes.text: You, Jerry, are the doofus.
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining who the real doofus is
jokes.punchline: Dramatic declaration of Jerry's doofus status
jokes.explanation: Simple role reversal with emphasis. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're the doofus!

jokes.text: No, no, no, that's from Star Trek III. / The Search for Spock. / Search for Spock?
jokes.setup: Elaine blaming Kramer's Star Trek wisdom
jokes.punchline: Kramer correcting which Star Trek movie, Elaine's exasperation
jokes.explanation: Callback to Star Trek theme with Kramer's pedantic correction. Shows his priorities even when being criticized.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This is all because of your Star Trek wisdom!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It was Star Trek: First Contact, not The Next Generation.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Kramer!

jokes.text: Jerry will tell you that the Wrath of Khan is the better picture, but for me, I-- / You doofus.
jokes.setup: Kramer starting Star Trek movie review
jokes.punchline: Elaine cutting him off with insult
jokes.explanation: Good timing on the interruption. Shows Kramer's inability to read the room and Elaine's frustration.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So I'm watching this Star Trek movie, right? And I'm thinking, this is pretty good stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Kramer, nobody cares about your Star Trek review.

jokes.text: She had a little change of plans. / What's going on? / Hey, Timmy, Clara. That was some kind of workout we had tonight, huh? Now we finish it.
jokes.setup: Children arriving for Kramer instead of mother
jokes.punchline: Revealing this was planned child ambush
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup and payoff for the children's revenge plot. The casual 'now we finish it' is perfectly ominous.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Who are all these kids?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Surprise! It's a child ambush!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: We planned the whole thing. Your mother was in on it the whole time.

jokes.text: Mama!
jokes.setup: Children attacking Kramer
jokes.punchline: Kramer's childlike cry for help
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the children's revenge. Kramer reduced to childlike state by actual children.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Giddy up!

jokes.text: What is that? / The urban sombrero. I put it on the cover. / Well, nobody sees the cover.
jokes.setup: Jerry seeing the catalogue cover
jokes.punchline: Jerry's weak attempt to minimize the damage
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Jerry's typical unhelpful optimism. Sets up the visual gag of the ridiculous cover.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not a big deal. It's just a catalogue cover. People see it for two seconds and then they throw it away.

jokes.text: What is that? / It's the new cover of the J. Peterman catalogue. It is Elaine's choice. Let's congratulate her.
jokes.setup: George seeing the urban sombrero cover
jokes.punchline: Kramer's sarcastic congratulations for the disaster
jokes.explanation: Kramer's deadpan sarcasm about Elaine's failure. Good character moment but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What is this? What am I looking at?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, congratulations. You've really done it this time.

jokes.text: Widower? Wait a second.
jokes.setup: Kramer calling George a widower
jokes.punchline: Jerry's delayed realization of the implication
jokes.explanation: Good delayed reaction moment. Jerry processing that George technically counts as a widower for dating purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, what? A widower? George, you're not a widower.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, technically...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God!

jokes.text: Your widower story has tested through the roof.
jokes.setup: Jerry doing 'research' on George's dating appeal
jokes.punchline: Treating tragedy like market research data
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry approach to relationships - treating them like business data. Dark but character-appropriate humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're saying that when a woman's boyfriend dies, that's actually a good time to approach her?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a window of opportunity. She's vulnerable, she's lonely...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You've really thought this through, haven't you?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, statistically speaking, more women are single after their boyfriends die.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're telling me is you've been studying the mortality rate of men whose girlfriends you want to date?

jokes.text: There's two really cute girls sitting at the counter eating grilled cheese. Cheese, George. Cheese.
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to lure George away from foundation meeting
jokes.punchline: Using George's cheese obsession as bait
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the cheese obsession theme. Jerry knowing exactly how to tempt George shows their friendship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, I got some really good news for you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I just found out they're having a sale on aged cheddar down at the market.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Aged cheddar? How aged are we talking?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Five years. Maybe more.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gone.

jokes.text: What do you say we go through this doll by doll.
jokes.setup: Foundation meeting cataloguing Susan's possessions
jokes.punchline: The tedious prospect of examining each individual doll
jokes.explanation: Good setup for George's continued torture. The mundane nature of the task adds to the comedy of his trapped situation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We have to go through all of Susan's things. Every single doll.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How many dolls are we talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hundreds. Thousands maybe. I don't know, it's going to take forever.

jokes.text: The next thing I know, my ass is out on the street.
jokes.setup: Man explaining how urban sombrero ruined his life
jokes.punchline: Crude description of getting fired
jokes.explanation: Shows the broader consequences of Elaine's catalogue disaster. Functional callback but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: She just walked up to me and said 'You're fired.' Just like that. No warning, no nothing. All because of that stupid sombrero I wore to the office.

dialogue.speaker: George Costanza
dialogue.line: I mean, what's the big deal? It's a hat. A festive hat. But apparently Mr. Pitt thought it was 'unprofessional.' So he tells my boss, my boss tells me to take it off, I refuse on principle, and boom - I'm out.

jokes.text: I never thought a hat would destroy my life.
jokes.setup: Man reflecting on his situation
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of being ruined by headwear
jokes.explanation: Nice summation of the urban sombrero's impact. The existential tone makes it funnier than it should be.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm a grown man, and my entire life has been destroyed by a hat. A hat! Do you know how pathetic that is? I wore it once, and now I'm persona non grata at every social gathering in Manhattan. My reputation is in ruins because of felt and a brim.

tmdb_overview: Susan's parents act to preserve her memory; Kramer gives Elaine a confidence boost after Peterman puts her in charge.
insight: Season premiere rebuilds momentum with 48 jokes across tight observational setups, hitting 80-point threshold.
